title: D to JK Flip-Flop Conversion
examTip: D→JK Conversion: D = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q. Requires AND-OR-NOT combinational logic with Q feedback. More complex than JK→D. K-map minimization confirms this is already in minimal form.

blocks.label: D = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q  (with Q feedback from D FF output)

theory:
Converting a D Flip-Flop to behave as a JK Flip-Flop requires adding an external combinational logic circuit that computes the proper D input from J, K, and the current output Q. Unlike the simple JK→D conversion (which needs only 1 NOT gate), this conversion needs two AND gates, one OR gate, and one NOT gate — plus Q output feedback.

Derivation using Characteristic Equations: The JK FF characteristic equation is Q(n+1) = J·Q-bar + K-bar·Q. The D FF has Q(n+1) = D. To make the D FF behave like a JK FF, we set: D = J·Q-bar + K-bar·Q. This means the D input to the D FF must be pre-computed from the external J, K signals and the current Q state.

Circuit Implementation: AND1 computes J·Q-bar (J ANDed with the complement of Q). AND2 computes K-bar·Q (the complement of K ANDed with Q). A NOT gate generates K-bar from K. The OR gate combines AND1 and AND2 outputs to produce D = J·Q-bar + K-bar·Q. This D value is then fed into the D FF, and the Q and Q-bar outputs are fed back to AND1 and AND2 respectively.

Verification via Truth Table: For J=0, K=0: D = 0·Q-bar + 1·Q = Q → No Change ✓. For J=0, K=1: D = 0·Q-bar + 0·Q = 0 → Reset ✓. For J=1, K=0: D = 1·Q-bar + 1·Q = Q-bar+Q = 1 → Set ✓. For J=1, K=1: D = 1·Q-bar + 0·Q = Q-bar → Toggle ✓.

K-map Analysis: The expression D = J·Q-bar + K-bar·Q is already in minimum sum-of-products form. No further simplification is possible, confirming that the two AND gates and one OR gate structure is optimal.
keyPoints: D = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q — requires AND-OR-NOT logic plus Q feedback
Circuit needs: 1 NOT gate, 2 AND gates, 1 OR gate (4 gates total vs 1 for JK→D)
Q and Q̄ outputs must be fed back to the input logic — creates sequential dependency
Verified for all 4 JK input combinations: NC, Reset, Set, Toggle all correct
K-map confirms D = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q is the minimal SOP expression
More complex than JK→D because D FF lacks the inherent memory feedback of JK FF
Useful when only D flip-flops are available but JK behavior is needed
useCases: Implementing JK counter behavior using D flip-flops in CMOS logic families
Digital systems coursework demonstrating flip-flop excitation table methodology
Understanding combinational feedback circuits and sequential logic state transitions
